narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away because of a model's prediction hits hard, especially when it flags a 46% chance of not passing the bar exam down the line. Picture the admissions team looking at your story through this lens: your Year 3 grades landing you squarely in the middle of the pack at decile 5 stood out as the biggest red flag, suggesting the rigors of law school might pull you down further from there. Your first-year decile of 5 echoed that concern, painting a picture of steady but not standout performance that the model saw as a strong pull toward struggling on the bar. Even with your upper-middle family income offering some stability, it somehow factored in as tilting the odds against success in this prediction. Your LSAT score of 30.5, while respectable, didn't shine brightly enough to counterbalance those trends, adding to the cautionary tale. Being white and planning full-time studies nudged things in a tougher direction too, while your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 kept the momentum toward that worrisome outcome. Interestingly, your gender as male was one of the few bright spots, gently pulling the prediction toward passing. It's this combination—middle-of-the-road deciles dominating the view—that made the model see higher risks ahead, not any single flaw, but the overall pattern of your academic path so far. You're clearly driven, going full-time and coming from a solid background, but the model worries those early plateaus might not ramp up in time for the bar's intensity. This isn't the end of your story; many with similar starts have pivoted by targeting bar prep early or bolstering weak areas. You've got the foundation—use this as fuel to rewrite what comes next. Hang in there; brighter paths await.
